title: [LLVM][Coroutines] Introduce TBAA metadata for coro frame object
author: yonillasky
description:
This should help the compiler determine that aliasing between frame objects and all other types is impossible.
There are cases where this AA issue can seriously harm the performance of compute intensive code running inside a coroutine, since it can break LICM and cause it to reload frame data inside tight loops over and over.

tests.test_body:
; Function Attrs: presplitcoroutine
define ptr @f(i32 %x) #0 {
entry:
  %id = call token @llvm.coro.id(i32 0, ptr null, ptr null, ptr null)
  %need.alloc = call i1 @llvm.coro.alloc(token %id)
  br i1 %need.alloc, label %dyn.alloc, label %begin

dyn.alloc:                                        ; preds = %entry
  %size = call i32 @llvm.coro.size.i32()
  %alloc = call ptr @malloc(i32 %size)
  br label %begin

begin:                                            ; preds = %dyn.alloc, %entry
  %phi = phi ptr [ null, %entry ], [ %alloc, %dyn.alloc ]
  %hdl = call ptr @llvm.coro.begin(token %id, ptr %phi)
  call void @print(i32 0)
  %0 = call i8 @llvm.coro.suspend(token none, i1 false)
  switch i8 %0, label %suspend [
    i8 0, label %resume
    i8 1, label %cleanup
  ]

resume:                                           ; preds = %begin
  call void @print(i32 %x)
  br label %cleanup

cleanup:                                          ; preds = %resume, %begin
  %mem = call ptr @llvm.coro.free(token %id, ptr %hdl)
  call void @free(ptr %mem)
  br label %suspend

suspend:                                          ; preds = %cleanup, %begin
  call void @llvm.coro.end(ptr %hdl, i1 false, token none)
  ret ptr %hdl
}

; Function Attrs: nounwind memory(argmem: read)
declare ptr @llvm.coro.free(token, ptr nocapture readonly) #1

; Function Attrs: nounwind memory(none)
declare i32 @llvm.coro.size.i32() #2

; Function Attrs: nounwind
declare i8 @llvm.coro.suspend(token, i1) #3

; Function Attrs: nocallback nofree nosync nounwind willreturn memory(argmem: read)
declare token @llvm.coro.id(i32, ptr readnone, ptr nocapture readonly, ptr) #4

; Function Attrs: nounwind
declare i1 @llvm.coro.alloc(token) #3

; Function Attrs: nounwind
declare ptr @llvm.coro.begin(token, ptr writeonly) #3

; Function Attrs: nounwind
declare void @llvm.coro.end(ptr, i1, token) #3

; Function Attrs: allockind("alloc,uninitialized")
declare noalias ptr @malloc(i32) #5

declare void @print(i32)

; Function Attrs: willreturn allockind("free")
declare void @free(ptr) #6

attributes #0 = { presplitcoroutine }
attributes #1 = { nounwind memory(argmem: read) }
attributes #2 = { nounwind memory(none) }
attributes #3 = { nounwind }
attributes #4 = { nocallback nofree nosync nounwind willreturn memory(argmem: read) }
attributes #5 = { allockind("alloc,uninitialized") "alloc-family"="malloc" }
attributes #6 = { willreturn allockind("free") "alloc-family"="malloc" }

@g_x1 = external global i32

; Function Attrs: presplitcoroutine
define ptr @f() #0 {
entry:
  %promise = alloca i32, align 4
  %buffer = alloca [128 x i8], align 1
  %x1.1 = load i32, ptr @g_x1, align 4, !tbaa !0
  %ref1 = getelementptr inbounds [128 x i8], ptr %buffer, i32 0, i32 %x1.1
  store i8 42, ptr %ref1, align 1
  %id = call token @llvm.coro.id(i32 0, ptr %promise, ptr null, ptr null)
  %need.alloc = call i1 @llvm.coro.alloc(token %id)
  br i1 %need.alloc, label %dyn.alloc, label %begin

dyn.alloc:                                        ; preds = %entry
  %size = call i32 @llvm.coro.size.i32()
  %alloc = call ptr @malloc(i32 %size)
  br label %begin

begin:                                            ; preds = %dyn.alloc, %entry
  %phi = phi ptr [ null, %entry ], [ %alloc, %dyn.alloc ]
  %hdl = call ptr @llvm.coro.begin(token %id, ptr %phi)
  store i32 111, ptr %promise, align 4
  call void @print(i32 0)
  %0 = call i8 @llvm.coro.suspend(token none, i1 false)
  switch i8 %0, label %suspend [
    i8 0, label %resume
    i8 1, label %cleanup
  ]

resume:                                           ; preds = %begin
  %the_111_from_earlier = load i32, ptr %promise, align 4, !tbaa !0
  call void @print(i32 %the_111_from_earlier)
  %x1.2 = load i32, ptr @g_x1, align 4, !tbaa !0
  %ref2 = getelementptr inbounds [128 x i8], ptr %buffer, i32 0, i32 %x1.2
  %result = load i32, ptr %ref2, align 4, !tbaa !0
  store i32 %result, ptr %promise, align 4, !tbaa !0
  %1 = call i8 @llvm.coro.suspend(token none, i1 true)
  switch i8 %1, label %suspend [
    i8 1, label %cleanup
  ]

cleanup:                                          ; preds = %resume, %begin
  %mem = call ptr @llvm.coro.free(token %id, ptr %hdl)
  call void @free(ptr %mem)
  br label %suspend

suspend:                                          ; preds = %cleanup, %resume, %begin
  call void @llvm.coro.end(ptr %hdl, i1 false, token none)
  ret ptr %hdl
}

; Function Attrs: nounwind memory(argmem: read)
declare ptr @llvm.coro.free(token, ptr nocapture readonly) #1

; Function Attrs: nounwind memory(none)
declare i32 @llvm.coro.size.i32() #2

; Function Attrs: nounwind
declare i8 @llvm.coro.suspend(token, i1) #3

; Function Attrs: nocallback nofree nosync nounwind willreturn memory(argmem: read)
declare token @llvm.coro.id(i32, ptr readnone, ptr nocapture readonly, ptr) #4

; Function Attrs: nounwind
declare i1 @llvm.coro.alloc(token) #3

; Function Attrs: nounwind
declare ptr @llvm.coro.begin(token, ptr writeonly) #3

; Function Attrs: nounwind
declare void @llvm.coro.end(ptr, i1, token) #3

; Function Attrs: allockind("alloc,uninitialized")
declare noalias ptr @malloc(i32) #5

declare void @print(i32)

; Function Attrs: willreturn allockind("free")
declare void @free(ptr) #6

attributes #0 = { presplitcoroutine }
attributes #1 = { nounwind memory(argmem: read) }
attributes #2 = { nounwind memory(none) }
attributes #3 = { nounwind }
attributes #4 = { nocallback nofree nosync nounwind willreturn memory(argmem: read) }
attributes #5 = { allockind("alloc,uninitialized") "alloc-family"="malloc" }
attributes #6 = { willreturn allockind("free") "alloc-family"="malloc" }

!0 = !{!1, !1, i64 0}
!1 = !{!"int", !2, i64 0}
!2 = !{!"omnipotent char", !3, i64 0}
!3 = !{!"Simple C++ TBAA"}

; Function Attrs: presplitcoroutine
define ptr @f(ptr %p) #0 {
entry:
  %x = load i32, ptr %p, align 4, !tbaa !0
  %id = call token @llvm.coro.id(i32 0, ptr null, ptr null, ptr null)
  %need.alloc = call i1 @llvm.coro.alloc(token %id)
  br i1 %need.alloc, label %dyn.alloc, label %begin

dyn.alloc:                                        ; preds = %entry
  %size = call i32 @llvm.coro.size.i32()
  %alloc = call ptr @malloc(i32 %size)
  br label %begin

begin:                                            ; preds = %dyn.alloc, %entry
  %phi = phi ptr [ null, %entry ], [ %alloc, %dyn.alloc ]
  %hdl = call ptr @llvm.coro.begin(token %id, ptr %phi)
  call void @print(i32 0)
  %0 = call i8 @llvm.coro.suspend(token none, i1 false)
  switch i8 %0, label %suspend [
    i8 0, label %resume
    i8 1, label %cleanup
  ]

resume:                                           ; preds = %begin
  call void @print(i32 %x)
  br label %cleanup

cleanup:                                          ; preds = %resume, %begin
  %mem = call ptr @llvm.coro.free(token %id, ptr %hdl)
  call void @free(ptr %mem)
  br label %suspend

suspend:                                          ; preds = %cleanup, %begin
  call void @llvm.coro.end(ptr %hdl, i1 false, token none)
  ret ptr %hdl
}

; Function Attrs: nounwind memory(argmem: read)
declare ptr @llvm.coro.free(token, ptr nocapture readonly) #1

; Function Attrs: nounwind memory(none)
declare i32 @llvm.coro.size.i32() #2

; Function Attrs: nounwind
declare i8 @llvm.coro.suspend(token, i1) #3

; Function Attrs: nocallback nofree nosync nounwind willreturn memory(argmem: read)
declare token @llvm.coro.id(i32, ptr readnone, ptr nocapture readonly, ptr) #4

; Function Attrs: nounwind
declare i1 @llvm.coro.alloc(token) #3

; Function Attrs: nounwind
declare ptr @llvm.coro.begin(token, ptr writeonly) #3

; Function Attrs: nounwind
declare void @llvm.coro.end(ptr, i1, token) #3

; Function Attrs: allockind("alloc,uninitialized")
declare noalias ptr @malloc(i32) #5

declare void @print(i32)

; Function Attrs: willreturn allockind("free")
declare void @free(ptr) #6

attributes #0 = { presplitcoroutine }
attributes #1 = { nounwind memory(argmem: read) }
attributes #2 = { nounwind memory(none) }
attributes #3 = { nounwind }
attributes #4 = { nocallback nofree nosync nounwind willreturn memory(argmem: read) }
attributes #5 = { allockind("alloc,uninitialized") "alloc-family"="malloc" }
attributes #6 = { willreturn allockind("free") "alloc-family"="malloc" }

!2 = !{!"omnipotent char", !3, i64 0}

comments.author: yonillasky
comments.body:
This change is not safe in all cases, I need to make it more cautious about which slots it annotates like this.
The data in a frame slot can be affected from user code if its address is ever taken.

Switching back to draft until it's fixed.

comments.author: yonillasky
comments.body:
The concern above should be taken care of now.
Setting to ready.

comments.author: NewSigma
comments.body:
I played with it and can confirm improvements in my personal benchmarks. Here are some observations for discussion:

1. Removing TBAA from StoreInst while retaining it on LoadInst seems do not impact performance in your demo. Is there a specific reason for adding TBAA on stores?
2. Can we find `TBAARoot` in `Shape::analyse` to avoid traversing the function?
3. Tests?

comments.author: yonillasky
comments.body:
1. Right, the issue is always with loads, stores are always generated to execute upon suspending and already can't be improved further. I can change it to apply only on loads.
2. I'll attempt to modify the impl to do that.
3. Yes, I will add/modify LIT tests to assert the metadata gets created, and specifically that alloca and promise fields do NOT get annotated with it.

comments.author: ChuanqiXu9
comments.body:
> I played with it and can confirm improvements in my personal benchmarks. Here are some observations for discussion:

Curious for your benchmark and the improvements of the PR.

comments.author: NewSigma
comments.body:
> Curious for your benchmark and the improvements of the PR.

Thanks for asking. The benchmark is adapted from [llvm-opt-benchmark](https://github.com/dtcxzyw/llvm-opt-benchmark) for my cases. With the patch applied, here are the IR differences that showed up and the redundant load is gone:

``` LLVM
define internal fastcc void @fn.resume(ptr noundef nonnull align 8 dereferenceable(72) %0) #33 personality ptr @__gxx_personality_v0 {
 cleanup31:
   %.reload.addr = getelementptr inbounds nuw i8, ptr %0, i64 40
-  %.reload = load <2 x float>, ptr %.reload.addr, align 8
+  %.reload = load <2 x float>, ptr %.reload.addr, align 8, !tbaa !177
   %.reload.addr39 = getelementptr inbounds nuw i8, ptr %0, i64 48
-  %.reload40 = load i64, ptr %.reload.addr39, align 8
+  %.reload40 = load i64, ptr %.reload.addr39, align 8, !tbaa !177
   %tobool.i.not.i.i = icmp eq i64 %.reload40, 0
   br i1 %tobool.i.not.i.i, label %CoroEnd, label %if.then.i.i

if.then.i.i:                                      ; preds = %cleanup31
  %x_.sroa.0.4.vec.extract21 = extractelement <2 x float> %.reload, i64 1
  %cmp.i.i.i = fcmp reassoc nsz oeq float %x_.sroa.0.4.vec.extract21, 0.000000e+00
  %1 = inttoptr i64 %.reload40 to ptr
  br i1 %cmp.i.i.i, label %if.end.invoke.i.i.from.if.then4.i.i, label %if.end.invoke.i.i.from.if.end.i.i

if.end.invoke.i.i.from.if.then4.i.i:              ; preds = %if.then.i.i
  %2 = getelementptr inbounds nuw i8, ptr %1, i64 8
  br label %if.end.invoke.i.i

 if.end.invoke.i.i.from.if.end.i.i:                ; preds = %if.then.i.i
   %3 = getelementptr inbounds nuw i8, ptr %1, i64 16
   %obj.sroa.0.0.vec.extract.i.i.i = extractelement <2 x float> %.reload, i64 0
-  store float %obj.sroa.0.0.vec.extract.i.i.i, ptr %3, align 4, !tbaa !127, !noalias !671
+  store float %obj.sroa.0.0.vec.extract.i.i.i, ptr %3, align 4, !tbaa !127, !noalias !673
   %g.i.i.i.i = getelementptr inbounds nuw i8, ptr %1, i64 20
-  store float %x_.sroa.0.4.vec.extract21, ptr %g.i.i.i.i, align 4, !tbaa !127, !noalias !676
-  %.reload38.pre = load i64, ptr %.reload.addr39, align 8
-  %.pre = inttoptr i64 %.reload38.pre to ptr
+  store float %x_.sroa.0.4.vec.extract21, ptr %g.i.i.i.i, align 4, !tbaa !127, !noalias !678
   br label %if.end.invoke.i.i
 
 if.end.invoke.i.i:                                ; preds = %if.end.invoke.i.i.from.if.end.i.i, %if.end.invoke.i.i.from.if.then4.i.i
-  %.pre-phi = phi ptr [ %.pre, %if.end.invoke.i.i.from.if.end.i.i ], [ %1, %if.end.invoke.i.i.from.if.then4.i.i ]
   %.in = phi ptr [ %1, %if.end.invoke.i.i.from.if.end.i.i ], [ %2, %if.end.invoke.i.i.from.if.then4.i.i ]
   %4 = load ptr, ptr %.in, align 8
-  invoke fastcc void %4(ptr nonnull %.pre-phi)
+  invoke fastcc void %4(ptr nonnull %1)
```

comments.author: yonillasky
comments.body:
I tried following the CR and make the code search for exactly `Simple C/C++ TBAA` or `Simple C++ TBAA`. However, it seems like there is no index of metadata strings that can be looked up like that. AI chat/agents are claiming that the only way to reach the metadata is by traversing instructions, which is basically what I did.

I will restrict my search to ignore roots with all other names, but it will still be in the "bad" form.
Do you know if there is any way the cost of this lookup could be minimized?

If there is a way to do it once for the module and then just send the MD node pointer into `insertSpills`, I won't need to even modify coro::Shape. Any ideas?

comments.author: yonillasky
comments.body:
Now that I've learned that C/C++ actually has two separate TBAA roots, I am reconsidering the implementation strategy.

It might be better to rebuild this as the following code changes:
- CoroSplit generates a new kind of MD ("CoroNoAliasSlots", "CoroNoAliasOffsets") that specifies which slots are safe to assume NoAlias on against non-frame-derived memops
- AA is extended to rely on this MD, when present, to prove NoAlias between a Gep/deref using a frame ptr, and unrelated memops.

WDYT?

comments.author: ChuanqiXu9
comments.body:
> I tried following the CR and make the code search for exactly `Simple C/C++ TBAA` or `Simple C++ TBAA`. However, it seems like there is no index of metadata strings that can be looked up like that. AI chat/agents are claiming that the only way to reach the metadata is by traversing instructions, which is basically what I did.
> 
> I will restrict my search to ignore roots with all other names, but it will still be in the "bad" form. Do you know if there is any way the cost of this lookup could be minimized?
> 
> If there is a way to do it once for the module and then just send the MD node pointer into `insertSpills`, I won't need to even modify coro::Shape. Any ideas?

In Metadata.h, we have `MDTuple *MDNode::getIfExists(LLVMContext &Context, ArrayRef<Metadata *> MDs);` and we have `MDString::get(LLVMContext &Context, StringRef Str)`, so I think we can write:

```C++
MDNode::getIfExists(C, MDString::get(C, "Simple C++ TBAA"));
```

note that `MDString::get(LLVMContext &Context, StringRef Str)` may create the MDString if the specified string is not exist. So maybe we want to implement `MDString::getIfExists`.

comments.author: ChuanqiXu9
comments.body:
> Now that I've learned that C/C++ actually has two separate TBAA roots, I am reconsidering the implementation strategy.
> 
> It might be better to rebuild this as the following code changes:
> 
> * CoroSplit generates a new kind of MD ("CoroNoAliasSlots", "CoroNoAliasOffsets") that specifies which slots are safe to assume NoAlias on against non-frame-derived memops
> * AA is extended to rely on this MD, when present, to prove NoAlias between a Gep/deref using a frame ptr, and unrelated memops.
> 
> WDYT?

I feel if TBAA can help us solving the problem, we don't need to reinvent the wheel.

comments.author: Dinistro
comments.body:
If we look at this from a more theoretical standpoint, then using TBAA for this is not the correct approach. What we are attempting to do in this draft is tricking the AA to believe that the underlying types are not the same (even though they are). While these objects can never alias, it's not type based information that guarantees this.
Furthermore, this only has any impact if the frontend was actually producing TBAA for the other memory operations in the first place. While clang does do some by default, there are many projects that deal with lower level code that disable this.

Given this, I would suggest to at least attempt a different approach. The correct way to model this might be relying on alias scopes. The drawback of this is that it requires adding metadata to **all** memory instructions in the functions.

comments.author: ChuanqiXu9
comments.body:
> If we look at this from a more theoretical standpoint, then using TBAA for this is not the correct approach. What we are attempting to do in this draft is tricking the AA to believe that the underlying types are not the same (even though they are). While these objects can never alias, it's not type based information that guarantees this. 

This may not be accurate. From C++'s perspective, the type of the coroutine frame of every coroutine function is a half opaque type. We can interpret this as the type of every coroutine frame is unique.

comments.author: yonillasky
comments.body:
From a practical standpoint, the main problems with the current draft now are:

- Relies on input program being tagged with TBAA in the first place

- Even for only C/C++ as a front end, it will not suffice to produce NoAlias in all cases we want, because there are two distinct TBAA roots. You have to use one of them when you tag the slot reloads, and then it won't produce NoAlias with other memops that are using the OTHER root (the one you haven't used)

I think "teaching" the AA itself to determine NoAlias with these slots makes more sense. It is analogous to the fact it has reasoning based on stack refs vs non-stack refs being NoAlias, regardless of types.

comments.author: yonillasky
comments.body: With that said, I'm also OK with merging this (it does provide some benefit) and then working on an alternative in another PR.

comments.author: ChuanqiXu9
comments.body:
> From a practical standpoint, the main problems with the current draft now are:
> 
> * Relies on input program being tagged with TBAA in the first place
> * Even for only C/C++ as a front end, it will not suffice to produce NoAlias in all cases we want, because there are two distinct TBAA roots. You have to use one of them when you tag the slot reloads, and then it won't produce NoAlias with other memops that are using the OTHER root (the one you haven't used)
> 
> I think "teaching" the AA itself to determine NoAlias with these slots makes more sense. It is analogous to the fact it has reasoning based on stack refs vs non-stack refs being NoAlias, regardless of types.

I don't think it is bad to teach AA more things. I just feel it is more complex. But as you're the author, I won't say no to that idea.

comments.author: ChuanqiXu9
comments.body:
> With that said, I'm also OK with merging this (it does provide some benefit) and then working on an alternative in another PR.

The current PR looks good to me if you can try the method I mentioned eariier to get the root by :

```
MDNode::getIfExists(C, MDString::get(C, "Simple C++ TBAA"));
```

comments.author: yonillasky
comments.body:
> > If we look at this from a more theoretical standpoint, then using TBAA for this is not the correct approach. What we are attempting to do in this draft is tricking the AA to believe that the underlying types are not the same (even though they are). While these objects can never alias, it's not type based information that guarantees this.
> 
> This may not be accurate. From C++'s perspective, the type of the coroutine frame of every coroutine function is a half opaque type. We can interpret this as the type of every coroutine frame is unique.

There is a sleight of hand in here.
Imagine that a coroutine has captured an int x and an int *p

the coroutine has a loop where it loads x and p, then does some RMW on *p.
If we did normal C++ TBAA typing of the coroutine frame, it would consider the reload of `x` (type int) as potentially aliasing the RMW on `*p` (type is also int). AA itself also may have a hard time proving `p` and `&frame->x` (both `int*`) do not alias. so LICM will not be able to move the reload of `x` out of the loop, even though it never changes.

The fake "scalar slot" type in this PR makes it so we don't hit this problem, but even this hack isn't quite enough, because of the two TBAA roots problem.

comments.author: ChuanqiXu9
comments.body:
> > > If we look at this from a more theoretical standpoint, then using TBAA for this is not the correct approach. What we are attempting to do in this draft is tricking the AA to believe that the underlying types are not the same (even though they are). While these objects can never alias, it's not type based information that guarantees this.
> > 
> > 
> > This may not be accurate. From C++'s perspective, the type of the coroutine frame of every coroutine function is a half opaque type. We can interpret this as the type of every coroutine frame is unique.
> 
> There is a sleight of hand in here. Imagine that a coroutine has captured an int x and an int *p
> 
> the coroutine has a loop where it loads x and p, then does some RMW on *p. If we did normal C++ TBAA typing of the coroutine frame, it would consider the reload of `x` (type int) as potentially aliasing the RMW on `*p` (type is also int). AA itself also may have a hard time proving `p` and `&frame->x` (both `int*`) do not alias. so LICM will not be able to move the reload of `x` out of the loop, even though it never changes.

I don't understand. Why  won't  x and p alias?

> 
> The fake "scalar slot" type in this PR makes it so we don't hit this problem, but even this hack isn't quite enough, because of the two TBAA roots problem.

The two TBAA roots you said is "Simple C++ TBAA" and "Simple C/C++ TBAA" ? If yes, I feel it might not be a problem.

comments.author: yonillasky
comments.body:
> I don't understand. Why won't x and p alias?

Because the address of `x` is never taken (if it was, it would become an alloca slot, and we specifically exclude such slots; let's say it isn't taken in my story)

And of course `p` could not be made to point to `frame->x` since the frame doesn't even exist before CoroSplit runs.
Yes, theoretically the user can clobber `frame->p` with a pointer to `frame->x` after creating the coro object, by blindly guessing the frame layout, but that code would already be broken contractually (the language makes no guarantees about the frame layout, none of the supported intrinsics allow this).

> The two TBAA roots you said is "Simple C++ TBAA" and "Simple C/C++ TBAA" ? If yes, I feel it might not be a problem.

Yes, they are, but if let's say you put the fake scalar in "Simple C/C++ TBAA", and there is an unrelated store of a type under "Simple C++ TBAA", then AA on them will return `MayAlias` because they belong to different TBAA roots (At least that is the claim by some AI, I will check myself later to confirm)

comments.author: ChuanqiXu9
comments.body:
> > I don't understand. Why won't x and p alias?
> 
> Because the address of `x` is never taken (if it was, it would become an alloca slot, and we specifically exclude such slots; let's say it isn't taken in my story)

Are you talking about `ByValTy`?  That works for argument only. Sorry could you elaborate on this story?

> 
> And of course `p` could not be made to point to `frame->x` since the frame doesn't even exist before CoroSplit runs. Yes, theoretically the user can clobber `frame->p` with a pointer to `frame->x` after creating the coro object, by blindly guessing the frame layout, but that code would already be broken contractually (the language makes no guarantees about the frame layout, none of the supported intrinsics allow this).
> 
> > The two TBAA roots you said is "Simple C++ TBAA" and "Simple C/C++ TBAA" ? If yes, I feel it might not be a problem.
> 
> Yes, they are, but if let's say you put the fake scalar in "Simple C/C++ TBAA", and there is an unrelated store of a type under "Simple C++ TBAA", then AA on them will return `MayAlias` because they belong to different TBAA roots (At least that is the claim by some AI, I will check myself later to confirm)

I guess, at least for C++, we don't need to worry for "Simple C/C++ TBAA".

comments.author: yonillasky
ChuanqiXu9
yonillasky
comments.body:
I think any variable that has its address taken becomes an alloca, then in CoroSplit it will be made into an "alloca slot" (which we don't put TBAA tags on).
That is why I don't care much about that case. Both in this PR and in the hypothetical case where we added C++-accurate TBAA for the frame structs we would get MayAlias in that case.

comments.author: ChuanqiXu9
comments.body:
> I think any variable that has its address taken becomes an alloca, then in CoroSplit it will be made into an "alloca slot" (which we don't put TBAA tags on). That is why I don't care much about that case. Both in this PR and in the hypothetical case where we added C++-accurate TBAA for the frame structs we would get MayAlias in that case.

Got it. So the impact of the patch is smaller than I feel initially. But I never say no to small improvements with small changes.

It looks like there are a lot of space we can improve in this area.
